title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Hi-lo actuator not moving or intermittent
description: Table not raising/lowering or intermittent hi-lo movement due to power, hand control, or connection-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove table from patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Unexpected table movement or failure can cause injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate the hi-lo function using the hand control or foot control. Expected: No movement, delayed response, or intermittent operation. Why it matters: Confirms a true actuator issue vs user operation error.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is plugged into a working outlet and power cord is intact. Test outlet with another device. Expected: Reliable power present. Why it matters: Loss of power is the most common cause of no movement. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Strain Relief
steps.instructions: Check for cuts, fraying, or loose connections at the plug and table entry point. Expected: Cord intact with no damage. Why it matters: Intermittent power can mimic actuator failure. If damaged → remove from service.

steps.title: Check Hand Control / Foot Control Function
steps.instructions: Inspect control for stuck buttons, physical damage, or inconsistent response. Try all movement buttons. Expected: Buttons respond consistently. Why it matters: Faulty controls are a common cause of intermittent movement. If available → swap with a known good control and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Control Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Verify that the hand/foot control cable is securely connected to the table with no bent pins or loose fit. Expected: Firm, secure connection. Why it matters: Loose or damaged connectors interrupt control signals. If connection is loose or damaged → reseat or remove from service.

steps.title: Observe for Actuator Noise or Attempted Movement
steps.instructions: Activate hi-lo and listen for motor noise or slight movement. Expected: Motor sound or movement attempt. Why it matters: Noise present → power/control reaching actuator. No noise → possible control or power issue.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect under the table for items blocking movement (cables, debris, storage items). Expected: Clear path for full actuator travel. Why it matters: Physical obstruction can stop or strain actuator movement. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Table Load and Weight Distribution
steps.instructions: Ensure table is not overloaded or unevenly loaded. Expected: Within normal operating weight and balanced. Why it matters: Excess load can prevent actuator movement or cause intermittent operation. If overloaded → reduce load and retest.

steps.title: Cycle Power to Reset System
steps.instructions: Unplug the table for 30 seconds, then reconnect and retest. Expected: Normal function restored if control fault was temporary. Why it matters: Resets control electronics and clears minor faults.

CCR.Complaint: Exam table not raising or lowering; intermittent response from hand control.
CCR.Cause: Hi-lo actuator unresponsive despite confirmed power and functional control.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to Clinical Engineering for actuator evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Audible actuator noise present or absent
Final device status (in service / out of service)

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Backrest not adjusting or drifting down
description: Troubleshooting guide for backrest not moving, drifting, or failing to hold position due to control, power, or mechanical issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Backrest instability can cause patient injury or sudden repositioning.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate the backrest using the hand control. Observe for no movement, delayed response, or drifting after adjustment. Expected: Backrest fails to move or cannot hold position. Why it matters: Confirms whether issue is control-related or mechanical drift.

steps.title: Check Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is plugged into a working outlet and has power. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Table powers on consistently. Why it matters: Insufficient power can prevent actuator operation. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Function
steps.instructions: Test all buttons on the hand control, especially backrest up/down. Expected: Buttons respond consistently. Why it matters: A faulty control can mimic actuator failure. If buttons are unresponsive or inconsistent → replace hand control and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable and Connections
steps.instructions: Check for loose connections, pinched wiring, or visible damage along the cable and connection port. Expected: Secure connection with no damage. Why it matters: Intermittent signals can cause erratic or no movement. If damaged or loose → reseat or replace and retest.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Response
steps.instructions: Press backrest controls and listen for motor noise from the actuator. Expected: Audible motor sound when activated. Why it matters: Helps distinguish electrical/control issues from mechanical failure. No sound: Possible control, wiring, or actuator failure. Sound but no movement: Likely mechanical issue or actuator slipping.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the backrest hinge area and linkage for debris, binding, or foreign objects. Expected: Smooth, unobstructed movement path. Why it matters: Obstruction can prevent movement or cause strain on actuator. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Backrest Support and Linkage
steps.instructions: Check pivot points, brackets, and mounting hardware for looseness or wear. Expected: All hardware secure with no excessive play. Why it matters: Worn or loose components can cause drifting or instability. If loose hardware → tighten and retest. If worn/damaged components → remove from service.

steps.title: Check for Hydraulic or Actuator Drift Behavior
steps.instructions: Raise the backrest and release the control. Observe if it slowly lowers on its own. Expected: Backrest holds position. Why it matters: Drifting typically indicates internal actuator or hydraulic failure. If drifting occurs → internal actuator failure likely.

CCR.Complaint: Backrest not adjusting and drifting down during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed backrest actuator causing inability to hold position.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for actuator replacement and repair.

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Hand control not responding
description: Hand control not activating table movement due to power, connection, or control-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Unexpected movement or loss of control can cause patient injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Press all buttons on the hand control and observe table response. Expected: No movement from any function or intermittent response. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is total control failure or isolated to specific buttons.

steps.title: Check Power to the Table
steps.instructions: Ensure the table is plugged into a known working outlet and powered on. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Table has consistent power. Why it matters: No power = no control response. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Check where the hand control plugs into the table (usually under the seat or base). Ensure it is fully seated. Expected: Secure, tight connection with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose connections are a common cause of full control failure. If loose → reseat connection and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control Cable Condition
steps.instructions: Trace the entire cable for cuts, pinching, kinks, or exposed wires. Pay close attention near strain relief points. Expected: Cable intact with no visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged cables interrupt signal transmission. If damaged → remove from service and replace hand control.

steps.title: Check for Pinched or Stretched Cable
steps.instructions: Look under the table and around moving parts where the cable may be caught or stretched during table movement. Expected: Cable moves freely without tension. Why it matters: Internal wire breakage often occurs from repeated pinching.

steps.title: Test Table with Alternate Control (if available)
steps.instructions: Swap with a known working hand control from another identical table. Expected: Table responds normally with alternate control. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is the hand control or internal table electronics. If alternate works → replace defective hand control.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Response
steps.instructions: While pressing buttons, gently move the cable near connectors. Expected: No change in function. Why it matters: Intermittent operation indicates internal wire failure.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Liquid Intrusion or Damage
steps.instructions: Check the hand control housing for cracks, sticky buttons, or fluid exposure. Expected: Clean, dry, intact housing. Why it matters: Fluid intrusion commonly causes button failure. If contamination is present → replace hand control.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported hand control not responding; table would not move.
CCR.Cause: Hand control cable found damaged near strain relief causing loss of signal.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced hand control and verified full table functionality.

helpfulDetails: Signs of fluid exposure or physical damage
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Paper Roll Holder or Headrest Mechanical Failure
description: Loose, broken, or non-functioning paper roll holder or headrest causing instability, inability to secure paper, or improper patient positioning.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Headrest or support failure can lead to patient discomfort or injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Inspect the paper roll holder and headrest for looseness, detachment, or improper function. Expected: Identify whether issue is structural, alignment-related, or hardware-related. Why it matters: Confirms exact failure point before adjustments.

steps.title: Inspect Paper Roll Holder Mounting Points
steps.instructions: Check brackets, rods, and mounting screws securing the paper roll holder. Expected: All hardware present, aligned, and tight. Why it matters: Loose or missing hardware is the most common cause of holder failure. If loose → tighten and retest If missing/damaged → replace hardware or remove from service

steps.title: Check Paper Roll Rod Condition and Fit
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect the paper roll rod for bending, warping, or improper seating. Expected: Rod is straight and fits securely in holders. Why it matters: A bent rod prevents proper rotation and paper feeding. If damaged → replace rod

steps.title: Inspect Headrest Adjustment Mechanism
steps.instructions: Operate the headrest adjustment (manual lever, friction joint, or ratcheting mechanism). Expected: Smooth movement with ability to hold position firmly. Why it matters: Worn or slipping mechanisms lead to drifting or instability. If slipping → check for loose fasteners or worn friction components

steps.title: Check Headrest Mounting and Support Structure
steps.instructions: Inspect hinges, brackets, and attachment points between headrest and table frame. Expected: No excessive play, wobble, or separation. Why it matters: Structural looseness directly impacts patient support. If loose → tighten hardware If cracked or bent → remove from service

steps.title: Inspect Upholstery and Padding Integrity (Headrest Area)
steps.instructions: Check for torn upholstery, compressed padding, or exposed internal structure. Expected: Surface intact and supportive. Why it matters: Damage may indicate deeper structural issues or create infection control concerns. If damaged → send for upholstery repair or replacement

steps.title: Check for Interference or Misalignment
steps.instructions: Ensure no components are obstructing headrest movement or paper roll rotation (covers, accessories, debris). Expected: Full, unobstructed range of motion. Why it matters: Misalignment can mimic mechanical failure.

steps.title: Functional Test
steps.instructions: Reinstall paper roll and operate headrest through full range. Expected: Paper feeds smoothly and headrest holds position without drift. Why it matters: Confirms issue resolution under normal use conditions. If resolved → return to service

CCR.Complaint: Paper roll holder loose and headrest not holding position during patient exams.
CCR.Cause: Loose mounting hardware at headrest hinge and misaligned paper roll bracket.
CCR.Resolution: Tightened hardware, realigned components, and verified proper function.

helpfulDetails: Presence of bent rod or brackets
Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Midmark Ritter 204 / 222 Series Exam Table - Power supply or internal wiring faults
description: Troubleshooting power loss or intermittent operation caused by external power or connection-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the table from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another table. Why it matters: Power instability can lead to unexpected movement or loss of positioning.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the table and operate all functions. Observe for no power, intermittent operation, or partial function. Expected: Confirm complete or intermittent loss of power. Why it matters: Determines if issue is total power failure or partial system issue.

steps.title: Check Power Source (Outlet)
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is plugged into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device if needed. Expected: Outlet provides consistent power. Why it matters: Power loss at the source is the most common and easiest issue to correct. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check the entire power cord for cuts, fraying, kinks, or damage. Ensure plug blades are intact and secure. Expected: Cord is intact with no visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged cords can cause intermittent or no power. If damage is found → remove from service and replace cord.

steps.title: Check Power Cord Connection at Table
steps.instructions: Inspect where the power cord enters the table base. Ensure it is fully seated and not loose. Expected: Secure connection with no movement. Why it matters: Loose connections can mimic internal wiring faults. If loose → reseat and retest.

steps.title: Inspect for Strain or Pinching
steps.instructions: Check if the power cord is pinched under the base, wheels, or caught in moving parts. Expected: Cord is free and not under stress. Why it matters: Pinched wiring can cause intermittent power loss. If pinched → reposition cord and retest.

steps.title: Check Circuit Breaker or Power Switch (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Locate any resettable breaker or main power switch on the table base. Reset if tripped. Expected: Breaker remains set and table powers on. Why it matters: Overcurrent conditions may trip protection and stop power. If breaker trips again → likely internal fault.

steps.title: Inspect Hand Control and Control Cable
steps.instructions: Ensure the hand control is properly connected and cable is not damaged. Expected: Secure connection and intact cable. Why it matters: Some tables will appear non-functional if control signal is lost. If damaged or loose → correct and retest.

steps.title: Observe for Signs of Internal Electrical Issues
steps.instructions: Look for burning smell, unusual heat, clicking, or intermittent power behavior. Expected: No abnormal signs present. Why it matters: These indicate likely internal wiring or power supply failure. If any signs are present → remove from service immediately.

CCR.Complaint: Table would not power on and had no response to hand control.
CCR.Cause: No external power issues found; suspected internal power supply or wiring fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to Clinical Engineering shop/vendor for repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual smell, heat, or sound

title: Midmark IQecg Series Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Patient Cable / Lead Failure
description: Troubleshooting no ECG signal, intermittent waveform, or excessive noise caused by faulty patient cables, lead wires, or poor connections.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Connect the patient cable and attempt to acquire a waveform. Observe for flatline, noise, or intermittent signal.

steps.title: Inspect Patient Cable and Leads (External Check)
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the main cable and individual lead wires for cracks, fraying, exposed wiring, or bent pins.

steps.title: Check Lead Wire Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure all lead wires are fully seated into the trunk cable and properly snapped or plugged in.

steps.title: Inspect Electrode Contact Points
steps.instructions: Check the ends of the lead wires (clips or snaps) for corrosion, residue, or wear.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Cable/Leads
steps.instructions: Swap the patient cable and/or lead set with a known working set.

steps.title: Check Connection to Acquisition Module
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient cable is fully seated into the ECG acquisition module (USB module).

steps.title: Observe for Artifact or Noise Sources
steps.instructions: Check for environmental or setup issues (patient movement, dried electrodes, poor skin prep).

CCR.Complaint: ECG not acquiring signal; leads reported as not working.
CCR.Cause: Patient cable found with intermittent internal break causing signal loss.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced patient cable with known-good unit; verified normal ECG waveform.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Midmark IQecg Series Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - ECG Not Acquiring Signal or Noisy Waveform
description: Troubleshooting poor or absent ECG signal, artifact, or excessive noise caused by lead issues, patient prep, cable faults, or environmental interference.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active use or switch to another monitor if clinically needed. Expected: Patient safely monitored by alternate equipment. Why it matters: Inaccurate ECG data can lead to improper clinical decisions.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to acquire an ECG and observe waveform quality. Expected: No signal, intermittent signal, or excessive noise/artifact. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is consistent and reproducible.

steps.title: Check Electrode Placement and Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect electrode placement and ensure proper positioning per standard lead configuration. Verify electrodes are fresh and not dried out. Expected: Electrodes properly placed with good adhesion. Why it matters: Poor skin contact is the most common cause of signal loss or noise. If electrodes are dried, loose, or expired → replace and retest.

steps.title: Prepare Patient Skin Properly
steps.instructions: Ensure skin is clean and dry. Shave excessive hair and lightly abrade skin if needed. Expected: Improved electrode contact and reduced impedance. Why it matters: Skin impedance directly affects signal quality.

steps.title: Inspect Lead Wires and Patient Cable
steps.instructions: Check for broken wires, fraying, bent pins, or loose connections at both ends. Expected: Cables intact with secure connections. Why it matters: Damaged leads can introduce noise or prevent signal acquisition. If damage is found → replace cable/lead set and retest.

steps.title: Verify Lead Connections to Module and PC
steps.instructions: Ensure patient cable is fully connected to the IQecg module and USB module is properly connected to the PC. Expected: Secure, fully seated connections. Why it matters: Loose connections can cause intermittent or no signal.

steps.title: Check for Electrical Interference
steps.instructions: Look for nearby equipment (IV pumps, beds, power cords) that may cause artifact. Move cables away from power sources. Expected: Reduction in waveform noise. Why it matters: Electromagnetic interference commonly introduces ECG artifact.

steps.title: Minimize Patient Movement
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient is still and relaxed during acquisition. Expected: Stable waveform with minimal artifact. Why it matters: Motion artifact can mimic noise or poor signal.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Swap electrodes and lead wires with a known working set. Expected: Signal improves with replacement accessories. Why it matters: Confirms whether issue is accessory-related vs device-related. If resolved → faulty accessories identified.

steps.title: Check Software Settings and Lead Selection
steps.instructions: Verify correct lead configuration and acquisition settings in the IQecg software. Expected: Proper waveform display for selected leads. Why it matters: Incorrect configuration can appear as signal loss.

CCR.Complaint: ECG machine not acquiring signal with excessive noise during patient use.
CCR.Cause: Dried electrodes and worn lead wires causing poor signal acquisition.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced electrodes and lead set; verified normal waveform acquisition.

helpfulDetails: Device returned to service or removed

title: Midmark IQecg Series Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - Software Not Launching or Freezing
description: Troubleshooting IQecg software not opening, freezing, or becoming unresponsive due to PC, connection, or software environment issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while connected to a patient. Patient moved to another functioning device. Delays in ECG acquisition can impact diagnosis and patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to launch the IQecg software and observe behavior (no launch, freeze, error message). Confirm exact failure mode to identify startup vs performance issues.

steps.title: Check PC Responsiveness
steps.instructions: Confirm the computer is functioning normally (mouse/keyboard responsive, other programs open). A frozen system can mimic software failure.

steps.title: Restart the Computer
steps.instructions: Perform a full system restart. Clears temporary memory issues and stuck processes.

steps.title: Verify USB Module Connection
steps.instructions: Check that the IQecg acquisition module is securely connected to the PC. Try a different USB port. Some versions require hardware detection for proper software function.

steps.title: Check for Multiple Instances of the Software
steps.instructions: Open Task Manager and look for multiple IQecg processes running. End any duplicate or unresponsive instances. Multiple instances can cause freezing or prevent proper launch.

steps.title: Confirm User Login and Permissions
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct user account is logged in and has proper permissions to run the software. Restricted permissions can block or limit application launch.

steps.title: Check for Recent Updates or Changes
steps.instructions: Ask if there were recent Windows updates, software updates, or configuration changes. Updates can introduce compatibility issues.

steps.title: Verify Software Shortcut and Installation Path
steps.instructions: Ensure the software is being launched from the correct shortcut or executable file. Broken shortcuts can prevent proper launch.

steps.title: Disable Background Interference
steps.instructions: Temporarily close unnecessary applications or antivirus scans that may be consuming system resources. Resource conflicts can cause freezing.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or Logs
steps.instructions: Document any error messages displayed during launch or freeze. Useful for escalation and vendor support.

CCR.Complaint: IQecg software would not launch and froze when opened.
CCR.Cause: Software process conflict and unresponsive background instance identified.
CCR.Resolution: Terminated duplicate processes, rebooted system, and verified normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or escalated)

title: Midmark IQecg Series Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - USB Module Not Recognized by PC
description: EKG USB acquisition module not detected by PC due to connection, driver, power, or port-related external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Disconnect the patient from the EKG system before troubleshooting. Expected: No active patient connected. Why it matters: Prevents inaccurate readings or electrical risk during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Connect the USB module and open the IQecg software. Observe if the device is detected. Expected: Module not listed or software shows no device. Why it matters: Confirms a true recognition issue versus user workflow error.

steps.title: Check USB Connection at Both Ends
steps.instructions: Inspect and reseat the USB cable at the module and PC. Ensure it is fully inserted. Expected: Secure, firm connection. Why it matters: Loose or partially connected USB cables are a common cause of detection failure. If loose → reseat and retest.

steps.title: Try a Different USB Port on the PC
steps.instructions: Move the USB cable to another known working USB port. Expected: Module is recognized on a different port. Why it matters: USB ports can fail or be disabled. If recognized → original port is faulty.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good USB Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the USB cable with a verified working cable of the same type. Expected: Device becomes recognized. Why it matters: Internal wire breaks in USB cables are common and not always visible. If resolved → replace faulty cable.

steps.title: Check PC Device Manager
steps.instructions: Open Device Manager and look for: Unknown USB device, Missing or unrecognized hardware. Expected: Device appears (even if flagged). Why it matters: Confirms whether the PC detects the hardware at a system level. If not present → possible cable, port, or module failure.

steps.title: Verify Driver Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure Midmark IQecg drivers/software are properly installed and up to date. Expected: No driver errors in Device Manager. Why it matters: Missing or corrupted drivers prevent proper device recognition. If drivers missing/corrupt → reinstall IQecg software and retest.

steps.title: Restart the PC and Software
steps.instructions: Restart the computer and relaunch IQecg software with the module connected. Expected: Device recognized after reboot. Why it matters: Clears USB communication faults and software conflicts.

steps.title: Test Module on Another PC
steps.instructions: Connect the USB module to a different PC with IQecg software installed. Expected: Module is recognized on alternate system. Why it matters: Isolates whether the issue is PC-related or module-related. If works on another PC → original PC configuration issue. If not → likely module failure.

steps.title: Inspect Module for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Check the USB module and connector for bent pins, cracks, or damage. Expected: No visible damage. Why it matters: Physical damage can prevent proper communication.

CCR.Complaint: EKG USB module not recognized by PC; unable to acquire ECG.
CCR.Cause: Failed USB acquisition module not detected on multiple PCs and ports.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair; replaced with working module.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (in service / out of service)

title: Midmark IQecg Series Electrocardiograph (EKG) Machine - EMR Integration or Data Export Failure
description: EKG studies not transferring to EMR, failed exports, or connectivity issues between IQecg software and clinical systems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no active patient study is being performed before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to export or send a completed test to the EMR and note any error messages.

steps.title: Confirm Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Check that the PC is connected to the network and test access to another network resource.

steps.title: Check EMR System Availability
steps.instructions: Confirm the EMR system is operational and accessible from other workstations.

steps.title: Verify Patient Demographics and Study Completion
steps.instructions: Ensure patient data is correct and the study is fully completed before export.

steps.title: Check Export/Interface Settings in IQecg Software
steps.instructions: Verify correct EMR destination, export format, and interface settings.

steps.title: Inspect USB Module and Device Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the acquisition module is properly connected and recognized by the PC.

steps.title: Restart Software and PC
steps.instructions: Close and reopen IQecg software or reboot the PC if needed.

steps.title: Check for Interface Engine or Middleware Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm interface engine is running and not showing errors.

CCR.Complaint: EKG studies not transferring to EMR from IQecg workstation.
CCR.Cause: Network cable disconnected from workstation preventing data transmission.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected network cable, verified connectivity, and confirmed successful EMR export.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (operational or removed from service)

title: Midmark M11 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Unit Not Reaching Temperature or Pressure
description: Cycle runs but fails to reach sterilization temperature/pressure due to water supply, loading, door seal, or external system issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from active use and ensure alternate sterilization methods are available. Expected: No instruments are delayed for patient care. Why it matters: Incomplete sterilization poses a direct patient safety risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe temperature/pressure readings and any alarms. Expected: Unit fails to reach setpoint or alarms out. Why it matters: Confirms a true performance issue and identifies when failure occurs.

steps.title: Check Water Reservoir Level and Quality
steps.instructions: Inspect the reservoir and ensure it is filled with distilled water to the proper level. Expected: Adequate clean water present. Why it matters: Low or poor-quality water prevents proper steam generation. If low/empty → refill and retest. If contaminated → drain, refill with distilled water, and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Door Seal (Gasket)
steps.instructions: Check the door gasket for cracks, debris, or improper seating. Clean if needed. Expected: Gasket is clean, intact, and sealing evenly. Why it matters: Steam leaks prevent pressure and temperature buildup. If damaged → remove from service and replace gasket.

steps.title: Verify Door Closure and Latching
steps.instructions: Ensure the door is fully closed and properly latched. Expected: Tight, even seal with no gaps. Why it matters: Improper closure prevents pressure from building. If not sealing → adjust closure or inspect latch alignment.

steps.title: Check Chamber Loading
steps.instructions: Inspect how instruments are loaded in the chamber. Avoid overloading or tightly packed trays. Expected: Proper spacing for steam circulation. Why it matters: Overloading restricts steam flow and prevents uniform heating. If overloaded → reduce load and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Drain Line and Filter
steps.instructions: Check the drain line for kinks or blockages and ensure the chamber drain filter is clean. Expected: Free-flowing drainage with no obstruction. Why it matters: Restricted drainage affects pressure regulation and cycle performance. If blocked → clear obstruction and retest.

steps.title: Check for Steam Leaks During Cycle
steps.instructions: Observe for visible steam escaping around the door or fittings during operation. Expected: No visible steam leaks. Why it matters: Steam loss directly reduces pressure and temperature. If leak is present → remove from service and escalate.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is plugged into a dedicated, functioning outlet with no interruptions. Expected: Stable power throughout the cycle. Why it matters: Inconsistent power can interrupt heating elements.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer not reaching temperature and failing cycles.
CCR.Cause: Low water level and debris on door gasket causing poor seal.
CCR.Resolution: Refilled reservoir, cleaned gasket, verified proper sealing, and confirmed normal cycle operation.

title: Midmark M11 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Door Not Sealing or Gasket Leaking
description: Door not sealing, visible steam leaks, failed cycles, or pressure loss caused by gasket wear, alignment, or closure issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from active use if cycle integrity is compromised. Expected: Backup sterilization method available. Why it matters: Improper sealing prevents sterilization and risks patient safety.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle or initiate a seal and observe for steam leaks, door gaps, or pressure alarms. Expected: Confirm visible leak, failure to pressurize, or door not sealing fully. Why it matters: Confirms a true sealing issue vs cycle or sensor-related fault.

steps.title: Inspect Door Gasket Condition
steps.instructions: Open the door and inspect the gasket for cracks, flattening, debris, or brittleness. Expected: Gasket is smooth, flexible, and intact. Why it matters: Worn or damaged gaskets are the most common cause of leaks. If damaged → replace gasket and retest. If dirty → clean and retest.

steps.title: Clean Gasket and Door Sealing Surface
steps.instructions: Wipe the gasket and mating surface using a damp, lint-free cloth. Remove debris or residue. Expected: Clean, smooth sealing surfaces with no obstruction. Why it matters: Even small debris can prevent a proper seal. If contamination is found → clean and retest.

steps.title: Check Door Alignment and Closure
steps.instructions: Close the door and ensure it seats evenly with no gaps. Observe latch engagement. Expected: Door closes flush and evenly with consistent pressure. Why it matters: Misalignment prevents full gasket compression. If uneven → inspect hinge area and alignment. If door appears warped → remove from service.

steps.title: Inspect Door Latch Mechanism
steps.instructions: Check latch handle and locking components for looseness, wear, or improper engagement. Expected: Latch fully engages and holds door securely. Why it matters: Incomplete latching prevents proper sealing pressure. If latch does not fully engage → adjust if possible or escalate.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions in Door Path
steps.instructions: Inspect chamber edge and door area for trays, debris, or buildup preventing closure. Expected: Clear path with no interference. Why it matters: Obstructions prevent full door closure and sealing. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Run Test Cycle After Corrections
steps.instructions: After cleaning, inspection, or adjustments, run a full cycle and monitor for leaks or alarms. Expected: No visible steam leaks and proper pressure achieved. Why it matters: Confirms issue resolution under operating conditions. If resolved → return to service.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer door leaking steam and failing cycles due to poor seal.
CCR.Cause: Door gasket worn and flattened, preventing proper seal.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced gasket, cleaned sealing surface, and verified proper operation with test cycle.

title: Midmark M11 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Cycle Aborts or Error Codes During Sterilization
description: Troubleshooting guide for sterilizer cycles aborting or showing error codes due to external causes like power, water, sealing, or loading issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not rely on this unit for active sterilization. Remove from service if needed. Expected: Alternate sterilization method available. Why it matters: Incomplete sterilization compromises patient safety.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle and observe when it aborts. Note any displayed error codes or alarms. Expected: Cycle stops at a repeatable stage with a specific error. Why it matters: Identifies whether failure is related to fill, heat, pressure, or door.

steps.title: Check Power Source Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is plugged into a dedicated, working outlet. Avoid power strips. Expected: Stable power with no interruptions. Why it matters: Voltage drops or interruptions can cause cycle aborts. If unstable power is found → correct and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Door Closure and Gasket
steps.instructions: Check that the door is fully closed and latched. Inspect gasket for debris, cracks, or misalignment. Expected: Tight seal with no visible gaps or damage. Why it matters: Poor sealing prevents pressure buildup and triggers aborts. If debris is found → clean and retest. If gasket damaged → replace before further use.

steps.title: Check Water Level in Reservoir
steps.instructions: Verify the reservoir is filled to the proper level with distilled water. Expected: Proper water level within marked range. Why it matters: Low water prevents proper steam generation and causes errors. If low → fill with distilled water and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Water Quality
steps.instructions: Confirm only distilled water is used. Look for mineral buildup or discoloration in the chamber. Expected: Clean chamber with no scaling. Why it matters: Poor water quality can affect sensors and heating efficiency. If buildup is present → clean chamber per facility protocol and retest.

steps.title: Check Chamber Loading and Tray Placement
steps.instructions: Ensure instruments are not overloaded and trays are properly seated. Expected: Proper spacing for steam circulation. Why it matters: Overloading restricts airflow and can trigger cycle faults. If overloaded → reduce load and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Drain and Filter Area
steps.instructions: Check for debris in the chamber drain or filter screen. Expected: Clear drain path with no obstruction. Why it matters: Blocked drainage affects pressure and cycle completion. If debris is found → clean and retest.

steps.title: Observe for Abnormal Sounds or Indicators
steps.instructions: Listen for unusual noises (clicking, no heating sound) and check indicator lights. Expected: Normal operation sounds and no persistent alarms. Why it matters: Helps identify heating or sensor-related failure patterns.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 30 seconds, then restart and run a test cycle. Expected: Unit resets and completes cycle normally. Why it matters: Clears temporary faults or control glitches.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer cycle aborting mid-cycle with error code during operation.
CCR.Cause: Low water level and debris found in drain restricting proper cycle function.
CCR.Resolution: Filled reservoir, cleaned drain/filter, and verified successful cycle completion.

title: Midmark M11 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Chamber Not Draining Properly
description: Troubleshooting guide for sterilizer chamber not draining due to blockage, setup issues, or drainage problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sterilizer from service and ensure no instruments are needed for immediate patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run a cycle or manually inspect the chamber after a completed cycle to confirm standing water or slow drainage.

steps.title: Check Installation and Drain Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is level and installed correctly with proper drain tubing routing.

steps.title: Inspect External Drain Tube
steps.instructions: Check for kinks, pinching, or improper elevation in the drain tube and correct as needed.

steps.title: Check Drain Container or Facility Drain Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the drain container is not full and facility drain is not blocked.

steps.title: Inspect Chamber Drain Strainer
steps.instructions: Remove and clean the chamber drain strainer to eliminate debris blockage.

steps.title: Check for Debris in Chamber Sump Area
steps.instructions: Inspect and clear any debris from the drain opening inside the chamber.

steps.title: Run a Test Cycle After Cleaning
steps.instructions: Run a full cycle to verify proper drainage and absence of standing water.

steps.title: Observe for Unusual Noises or Delayed Drain Behavior
steps.instructions: Listen for abnormal sounds or delays during draining which may indicate internal issues.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer chamber not draining; standing water present after cycles.
CCR.Cause: Drain strainer clogged with debris restricting water flow.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned strainer, cleared drain path, and verified proper drainage during test cycle.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual noises during drain phase

title: Midmark M11 UltraClave Series Sterilizer - Control Board or Keypad Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for unresponsive keypad or control board issues caused by external power, contamination, or interface-related factors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure no instruments needed for immediate patient care are dependent on this unit. Expected: Backup sterilization method in place. Why it matters: Loss of sterilization capability impacts patient safety and workflow.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the unit and press multiple keypad buttons. Observe display response and cycle initiation. Expected: No response, delayed response, or incorrect inputs. Why it matters: Confirms true keypad/control issue vs user error.

steps.title: Check Power to the Unit
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is plugged into a known working outlet. Check circuit breaker if applicable. Expected: Display powers on consistently. Why it matters: Low or unstable power can mimic control or keypad failure. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Display and Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe the screen for partial display, flickering, or error messages. Expected: Clear, stable display. Why it matters: Display issues may indicate broader control board problems rather than keypad alone.

steps.title: Check for Keypad Obstruction or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect keypad surface for debris, moisture, or chemical residue. Expected: Clean, dry, and unobstructed buttons. Why it matters: Residue or moisture can prevent proper button contact. If contamination is present → clean per facility guidelines and retest.

steps.title: Test Individual Keypad Buttons
steps.instructions: Press each button and observe if some respond while others do not. Expected: Consistent response across all buttons. Why it matters: Partial failure suggests keypad membrane issue rather than full control board failure. If only some buttons fail → likely keypad membrane issue.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, unplug it for 30–60 seconds, then power back on. Expected: System reboots and restores normal function. Why it matters: Clears temporary software or control logic faults. If resolved → monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections (If Accessible)
steps.instructions: Check any accessible external connectors (e.g., keypad ribbon entry point if visible without disassembly). Expected: Secure and undamaged connections. Why it matters: Loose or partially connected interfaces can cause intermittent control issues.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Assess for excessive humidity, recent cleaning exposure, or fluid ingress near the control panel. Expected: Dry, normal operating environment. Why it matters: Moisture intrusion is a common cause of keypad malfunction.

CCR.Complaint: Sterilizer keypad unresponsive; unable to start cycle.
CCR.Cause: Keypad buttons non-responsive after cleaning and power cycle; likely keypad membrane failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and sent for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Specific buttons working vs not working
Presence of moisture or residue on keypad
Final device status (removed from service or operational)

title: Midmark 630 / 641 Series Procedure Chair - Hi-Lo or Tilt Actuator Failure
description: Troubleshooting a procedure chair that will not raise, lower, or tilt due to power, control, or connection-related issues before assuming actuator failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the chair from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another chair. Why it matters: Loss of movement or sudden position change can cause injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate both hi-lo and tilt functions using the hand/foot control. Expected: No movement, partial movement, or intermittent response. Why it matters: Determines if issue is isolated (one function) or system-wide.

steps.title: Check Power Source (Outlet)
steps.instructions: Confirm the chair is plugged into a known working outlet. Test outlet with another device. Expected: Reliable power present. Why it matters: Power issues are the most common cause of actuator failure symptoms. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Entry Point
steps.instructions: Check for cuts, fraying, or loose connections where the cord enters the chair base. Expected: Cord intact and secure. Why it matters: Damaged cords can cause intermittent or complete loss of actuator function. If damaged → remove from service.

steps.title: Check Control Interface (Hand/Foot Control)
steps.instructions: Operate all buttons and observe if any functions respond. Expected: Consistent response from all controls. Why it matters: A failed control can mimic actuator failure. If no buttons respond → suspect control or connection issue.

steps.title: Inspect Control Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Verify the hand/foot control cable is securely connected to the chair (typically under base or rear panel). Expected: Firm, fully seated connection. Why it matters: Loose or partially connected cables prevent actuator commands. If loose → reseat and retest.

steps.title: Check for Pinched or Damaged Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect visible wiring around moving parts (base, pivot points). Expected: No pinching, cuts, or exposed wires. Why it matters: Movement of the chair can damage cables over time, interrupting signals.

steps.title: Listen for Actuator Response
steps.instructions: Activate hi-lo or tilt while listening for motor noise. Expected: Audible motor operation when function is pressed. Why it matters: Noise present but no movement → possible mechanical binding or actuator failure. No noise → likely electrical/control issue.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect under the chair and around linkage points for objects or debris blocking movement. Expected: Clear movement path. Why it matters: Physical obstruction can prevent actuator travel and mimic failure. If obstruction found → remove and retest.

steps.title: Test Alternate Functions
steps.instructions: Check if other powered functions (backrest, leg section, rotation if applicable) work. Expected: Other functions operate normally. Why it matters: If all functions fail → likely power/control issue. If only one fails → likely specific actuator or localized issue.

CCR.Complaint: Chair would not raise or tilt during patient setup.
CCR.Cause: Hi-lo actuator unresponsive after verifying power, controls, and connections.
CCR.Resolution: Removed chair from service and sent for actuator replacement evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Presence or absence of motor noise
Any visible cable damage or pinching

title: Midmark 630 / 641 Series Procedure Chair - Foot Control or Hand Control Not Responding
description: Troubleshooting guide for procedure chair controls not responding due to power, connection, or accessory-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the chair from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated. Why it matters: Loss of control can result in unsafe positioning or sudden movement.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate the chair using both the hand control and foot control (if equipped). Expected: No response or intermittent movement. Why it matters: Determines if issue is isolated to one control or system-wide.

steps.title: Check Power to the Chair
steps.instructions: Confirm the chair is plugged into a known working outlet. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Chair has consistent power (indicator lights if applicable). Why it matters: No power = no control response. If no power → restore power and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connection
steps.instructions: Check for loose plugs, damaged cords, or strain at entry points. Expected: Cord intact and securely connected. Why it matters: Intermittent power can mimic control failure.

steps.title: Check Control Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect where the hand control and/or foot control connect to the chair (typically under the seat or base). Ensure connectors are fully seated. Expected: Secure, tight connections with no looseness. Why it matters: Loose or partially connected cables are a common failure point. If loose → reconnect and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Control Cables for Damage
steps.instructions: Run hands along cables checking for cuts, pinches, or exposed wires. Expected: No visible damage. Why it matters: Damaged cables interrupt signal transmission. If damaged → remove from service and replace control.

steps.title: Test Each Control Independently
steps.instructions: If both hand and foot controls are present, test each separately. Expected: At least one control functions normally. Why it matters: Helps isolate whether the issue is with a specific control or the chair system. If one works → faulty control accessory. If neither works → continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Mechanical Binding
steps.instructions: Ensure nothing is preventing chair movement (base obstruction, debris, etc.). Expected: Chair moves freely when activated. Why it matters: Mechanical resistance may appear as control failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Chair
steps.instructions: Unplug the chair, wait 10–15 seconds, then reconnect power. Expected: Controls respond after reset. Why it matters: Resets control system and clears minor faults.

CCR.Complaint: Chair not responding to hand or foot control during patient positioning.
CCR.Cause: Loose hand control cable connection under chair base.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected cable and verified full chair functionality.

helpfulDetails: Control type affected (hand, foot, or both)
Any indicator lights present or absent
Any unusual sounds or lack of motor activity
Final device status (functional or removed from service)

title: Midmark 630 / 641 Series Procedure Chair - Chair Drifting or Not Holding Position
description: Troubleshooting guide for a procedure chair drifting or not holding position due to control, load, or external issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the chair from active patient use before troubleshooting. Expected: Patient safely relocated. Why it matters: Uncontrolled movement can lead to patient injury.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Operate all chair functions (hi-lo, tilt, back, leg) and observe for drifting after stopping movement. Expected: Drift occurs in a specific section or overall. Why it matters: Identifies whether the issue is isolated or system-wide.

steps.title: Check for Overloading or Uneven Weight Distribution
steps.instructions: Confirm patient weight is within rated limits and centered properly on the chair. Expected: Weight evenly distributed within limits. Why it matters: Overloading or uneven load can cause actuators to back-drive or fail to hold. If overloaded → correct and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Hand and Foot Controls
steps.instructions: Check for stuck buttons or continuous activation (especially on foot controls). Expected: No buttons stuck or unintentionally pressed. Why it matters: A stuck control can simulate continuous movement, causing drift. If stuck → clean, reposition, or replace control and retest.

steps.title: Check Control Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect connections for hand and foot controls at the chair base. Ensure they are fully seated and undamaged. Expected: Secure, intact connections. Why it matters: Intermittent signals can cause unintended actuator movement. If loose/damaged → reseat or replace and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Chair for External Mechanical Issues
steps.instructions: Look for loose bolts, worn pivot points, or misalignment in moving sections. Expected: All joints tight and aligned. Why it matters: Mechanical looseness can mimic actuator drift or allow movement after positioning. If loose → tighten hardware and retest.

steps.title: Cycle the Chair Through Full Range of Motion
steps.instructions: Run all movements fully up/down and back/forth several times. Expected: Smooth operation with consistent stopping points. Why it matters: Helps reset positioning and identify inconsistent actuator behavior. If drift resolves → monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Check Power Stability
steps.instructions: Ensure the chair is plugged into a reliable outlet with no intermittent power loss. Expected: Stable power supply. Why it matters: Voltage instability can affect actuator holding performance. If unstable → correct power source and retest.

CCR.Complaint: Chair drifting down after positioning during patient setup.
CCR.Cause: No external issues found; likely internal actuator unable to hold position.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to vendor for actuator evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Load conditions at time of issue
Presence of unusual noise during drift
Final device status (removed from service or operational)

title: Midmark 630 / 641 Series Procedure Chair - Upholstery Damage Affecting Positioning / Support
description: Damaged or compromised upholstery affecting patient support, positioning, and infection control.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the chair from active patient use before inspection. Expected: Patient safely relocated to another chair. Why it matters: Damaged upholstery can compromise patient stability and increase risk of injury or infection.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Inspect the affected area for tears, compression, or separation. Expected: Visible damage or loss of cushioning integrity. Why it matters: Confirms whether the issue is cosmetic or affecting support and positioning.

steps.title: Assess Severity of Upholstery Damage
steps.instructions: Check for surface cracks, deep tears exposing foam, or flattened cushioning. Expected: Clear understanding of damage level. Why it matters: Structural cushion failure impacts support. If minor wear → monitor. If foam exposed → proceed.

steps.title: Check Cushion Integrity and Support
steps.instructions: Apply pressure and compare with unaffected areas. Expected: Even resistance. Why it matters: Collapsed foam affects positioning. If uneven → replacement required.

steps.title: Inspect Upholstery Attachment Points
steps.instructions: Check seams and mounting points. Expected: Secure attachment. Why it matters: Loose upholstery affects safety. If loose → escalate for replacement.

steps.title: Evaluate Infection Control Risk
steps.instructions: Check if surface can be properly cleaned. Expected: Smooth and intact. Why it matters: Damaged surfaces harbor contaminants. If not cleanable → remove from service.

steps.title: Check for Associated Mechanical Impact
steps.instructions: Ensure damage is not interfering with movement. Expected: Full range of motion. Why it matters: Material can catch and worsen damage. If interference present → remove from service.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported torn seat upholstery causing patient discomfort and instability during procedures.
CCR.Cause: Seat cushion upholstery torn with exposed foam and loss of structural support.
CCR.Resolution: Removed chair from service and sent for upholstery replacement.

helpfulDetails: Location of damage (seat, backrest, armrest, leg section)
Depth of tear (surface vs exposed foam)
Final device status (in service vs out of service)

title: Midmark 630 / 641 Series Procedure Chair - Power Supply or Internal Wiring Faults
description: Chair not powering on, intermittent operation, or loss of functions due to power source, cord, or connection-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the chair from active patient use before troubleshooting. Patient safely relocated to another chair.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the chair and operate all functions to confirm no power, intermittent power, or partial function loss.

steps.title: Check Power Source (Outlet)
steps.instructions: Confirm the chair is plugged into a known working outlet and test with another device. Restore power if needed.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check for cuts, fraying, or bent prongs. Replace cord if damaged.

steps.title: Check Power Entry Point on Chair
steps.instructions: Inspect the cord entry point at the base for secure connection and no strain.

steps.title: Look for Indicator Lights or Sounds
steps.instructions: Check for control panel lights or relay clicks indicating power presence.

steps.title: Test Hand/Foot Controls
steps.instructions: Attempt to operate all controls and observe for response. Partial function may indicate non-total power failure.

steps.title: Check for Tripped Breakers or Power Strips
steps.instructions: Verify any external power strips or circuits have not tripped and are supplying continuous power.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Internal Electrical Issues
steps.instructions: Look for burning smell, discoloration, or heat. Remove from service immediately if present.

CCR.Complaint: Procedure chair would not power on and was non-responsive to controls.
CCR.Cause: Fault traced to damaged power cord at strain relief causing intermittent power loss.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced power cord and verified full chair functionality.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds, heat, or smell
